prompt: Which choice best describes the overall structure of the passage?

choices.text: It lists, in order, the steps a diver should follow to observe an octopus.
choices.rationale: No how-to steps are given.

choices.text: It argues that octopuses are endangered and should be protected from fishing.
choices.rationale: The passage makes no argument about protection.

choices.text: It compares two different species of octopus and weighs their abilities.
choices.rationale: Only one animal is discussed, not two species.

choices.text: It introduces a surprising trait, explains its mechanism, then notes a deeper puzzle.
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage moves from a striking fact to a mechanism to the colorblindness paradox.

explanation: The passage's purpose is to explain and then deepen a surprising fact, not to argue or instruct.

stimulus.title: The Color of Thought
stimulus.text: An octopus has no bones and, oddly, almost no color of its own. Yet in less than a second it can flush deep red with alarm or fade to match the speckled gray of a stone. Beneath its skin lie millions of tiny sacs of pigment, each ringed by muscle. When the muscles pull, the sacs widen and color blooms; when they relax, the color vanishes. Stranger still, the octopus is colorblind, so it cannot see the very hues it produces. Some researchers suspect its skin itself senses light, letting the animal paint a disguise it can never admire.

prompt: The passage develops its main point primarily by

choices.text: giving the reader directions for drawing an accurate map
choices.rationale: No drawing directions are given.

choices.text: listing the names of the era's most famous sea explorers
choices.rationale: No explorers are named.

choices.text: comparing two competing modern digital mapping apps
choices.rationale: No modern apps are compared.

choices.text: contrasting early guesswork with the later, corrected knowledge
choices.rationale: Correct. It sets blank-space guesses against corrections from exploration.

explanation: The structure contrasts early guesses with later corrections.

stimulus.title: The Edge of the Map
stimulus.text: Old maps are full of guesses. Where knowledge ran out, mapmakers filled the blank spaces with mountains they had never seen and coastlines that bent the wrong way. Some added sea monsters, less from belief than from a need to decorate the unknown. As explorers returned with measurements, the guesses were slowly corrected, and the monsters swam off the page. But a finished, accurate map can hide how hard it was to make. Every clean line was once a question, settled only by someone willing to sail past the edge of what was known and come back with an answer.

prompt: The passage is structured mainly as

choices.text: a numbered list of tips for writing a letter home
choices.rationale: No tips are listed.

choices.text: a sequence of three drafts leading to a final realization
choices.rationale: Correct. It moves through the three versions to Tomas's closing insight.

choices.text: a comparison of two different cities Tomas has lived in
choices.rationale: Two cities are not compared in detail.

choices.text: a back-and-forth argument between Tomas and a friend
choices.rationale: There is no argument between characters.

explanation: The arc runs through three drafts to a final realization.

stimulus.title: The Unsent Letter
stimulus.text: Tomas wrote the letter three times before he stopped trying to send it. In the first version he was cheerful, listing the small triumphs of his new city as if homesickness were a thing he had outgrown. In the second he was honest, and the page filled with everything he missed until it embarrassed him. The third version said almost nothing at all. He kept that one folded in a drawer. Months later he found it again and realized the words he could not send had still done their work: writing them had let him admit, if only to himself, how far from home he truly felt.

prompt: The passage develops its main idea primarily by

choices.text: comparing two rival seed vaults located in different regions
choices.rationale: No rival vault is compared.

choices.text: providing recipes that make use of the crops stored inside
choices.rationale: No recipes appear.

choices.text: explaining the vault's purpose and how its cold preserves the seeds
choices.rationale: Correct. It describes the vault's backup purpose and the role of cold.

choices.text: listing, one by one, the many countries that helped build it
choices.rationale: No countries are listed.

explanation: The structure explains purpose and preserving mechanism.

stimulus.title: A Vault for Seeds
stimulus.text: Deep inside a frozen mountain, a vault holds something more valuable than gold: seeds. Hundreds of thousands of varieties of crops, gathered from around the world, rest in sealed packets at temperatures far below freezing. The cold slows the seeds' aging to a crawl, so that even after decades they can still sprout. The vault is a backup. If a war, flood, or disease wipes out a crop somewhere, a sample can be withdrawn and grown again. Its keepers hope it will rarely be needed, the way one hopes never to use a fire extinguisher. Its value lies precisely in being ready and almost never used.

prompt: The passage is organized mainly as a shift from

choices.text: a back-and-forth debate between two keepers
choices.rationale: There is no debate between characters.

choices.text: a human-run past to an automated present
choices.rationale: Correct. It moves from the era of keepers to the era of automation.

choices.text: a set of instructions for building a tower
choices.rationale: No building instructions appear.

choices.text: a list of the world's most famous lighthouses
choices.rationale: No locations are listed.

explanation: The structure contrasts a manual past with an automated present.

stimulus.title: Keeping the Light
stimulus.text: For centuries, a lighthouse meant a keeper. Someone had to climb the tower each night, trim the wick, polish the lens, and wind the clockwork that turned the light. Storms made the work dangerous, and isolation made it lonely, but ships depended on that faithful beam. In the twentieth century, automation arrived. Electric lamps, timers, and remote sensors took over the tasks one by one, and keepers were gradually withdrawn. The lights still shine, more reliably than ever, yet something human slipped away with the last keeper. The machines kept the promise of the light; they could not keep its company.

choices.text: a moment of doubt that gradually gives way to a realization
choices.rationale: Correct. It moves from Nadia's hesitation to her concluding insight.

choices.text: a numbered list of tips for choosing a good violin
choices.rationale: No tips are offered.

choices.text: a back-and-forth debate between two rival musicians
choices.rationale: There is no debate between characters.

choices.text: a chronological history of one town's orchestra
choices.rationale: It is a single scene, not an orchestra's history.

explanation: The arc runs from doubt to epiphany.

stimulus.title: The Borrowed Violin
stimulus.text: Nadia had practiced on a school instrument so battered that two of its pegs slipped during every lesson. When her teacher handed her a loaner violin for the recital, she nearly refused; it felt wrong to play something so fine. But as she drew the bow across the strings, the tone opened like a window onto a bright street. Notes she had fought for all year now arrived easily, as if the instrument had been waiting for her. Afterward, returning it to its case, she understood that her old struggles had not been wasted. The fine violin had simply revealed the player she had quietly become.

prompt: The author contrasts 'this ideal' with 'reality' mainly to

choices.text: argue that glass, on balance, should not be recycled at all
choices.rationale: The author supports recycling.

choices.text: highlight the gap between what is possible and what actually happens
choices.rationale: Correct. The contrast spotlights the gap the author wants fixed.

choices.text: compare glass with plastic across several points of difference
choices.rationale: Plastic is not compared in detail.

choices.text: describe in detail the process by which new glass is manufactured
choices.rationale: Manufacturing is not described.

explanation: The ideal-versus-reality contrast frames the central problem.

stimulus.title: The Endless Material
stimulus.text: Glass has a quiet superpower: it can be melted down and remade again and again without losing quality. A bottle recycled today can become a bottle next month, then another after that, in a loop that, in principle, never wears out. Compared with this ideal, reality lags. Much glass still ends up in landfills because collection is uneven and because mixing colors lowers the value of the recycled material. Sorting by color and keeping glass out of general trash would let more of it re-enter the loop. The material is willing; it is our systems, the author suggests, that need redesigning.

prompt: Which choice best describes how the passage is organized?

choices.text: It compares two different marshes located on opposite coasts.
choices.rationale: No two marshes are compared.

choices.text: It gives instructions for restoring a marsh that has been drained.
choices.rationale: No restoration instructions are given.

choices.text: It narrates the personal story of one person who lived by a marsh.
choices.rationale: No personal story is told.

choices.text: It overturns a first impression by detailing the marsh's hidden benefits.
choices.rationale: Correct. It begins with the marsh looking like wasteland, then reveals its value.

explanation: The passage moves from a dismissive first impression to the marsh's real worth.

stimulus.title: The Quiet Buffer
stimulus.text: Where the land meets the sea, a salt marsh can look like wasteland: flat, muddy, and crossed by winding channels. Yet this drab landscape does remarkable work. Its dense grasses slow incoming waves, blunting the force of storms before they reach homes inland. The same grasses trap sediment, letting the marsh build itself upward as the sea slowly rises. Beneath the mud, roots store carbon that might otherwise warm the air. For years, marshes were drained and filled without much thought. Only later did people recognize that the unglamorous marsh had been protecting them all along, asking nothing in return.
